segments.text:
Cancer:
Role of Molecular Genetics in Discovering the Genetic Mechanisms

The Genetics of Cancer
The discovery of genes that cause cancer using transforming viruses
Viral cancer genes have normal human counterparts
The normal human genes are involved in cell growth control
Dominant Cancer Genes (Oncogenes)
Recessive Cancer Genes (Tumor suppressor genes/inhibit cancer 	progression)
Cancer is a multi-step process, and cancer cells contain more than one primary lesion.

Rous Sarcoma virus
1910 Peyton Rous could transplant sarcomas (cancer of the connective or muscle tissue) from chicken to chicken
Cell free filtrates could also induce tumor development
Host cell

Rous Sarcoma Virus
gag-precursor protein that is cleaved to produce virus particle 	proteins
pol-encodes a precursor that is cleaved to produce reverse 	transcriptase and an enzyme needed for the integration of 	the proviral DNA into the host cell chromosome
env-precursor to the envelope glycoprotein

td Rous Sarcoma mutants
td transformation defective
wild type
(Tumor causing)

Oncogene
A gene whose action promotes cell proliferation
Are dominant
Src Protein Structure

Activation of Src kinase
V-src is missing C terminus, and the inhibitory phosphorylation site, so it is constitutively active
Src protein kinase acts as an integrating device

src substrates include receptor tyrosine kinases and
proteins involved in cell morphology, motility and adhesion
Frame 2002
Tumor viruses
Viruses Associated with Human Cancers

The Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 1989
"for their discovery of the cellular origin of retroviral oncogenes"

Proto-oncogene
Normal gene, usually concerned with the regulation of cell proliferation, that can be converted into a cancer promoting oncogene by mutation.

Proto-oncogene
mRNA is reverse transcribed and gets inserted into retroviral genome
Transformation deficient  (Td) RSV

Direct Isolation of Human Oncogenes
Tumor cells
Isolate Total Cellular DNA
Partial Restriction Digest
Ligate marker gene

Direct Isolation of Human Oncogenes
Marked genes
Transform into mouse host cells
Isolate total DNA
Re-transform mouse host cells
Re-isolate DNA
Package into virus
Isolate viral plaques with marker
Isolation of Human Oncogenes

Activation of human proto-oncogenes
By “activation” it means that the proto-oncogene is being “converted” to an oncogene
Oncogenes are best understood within the context of normal cell growth controls

Classes of proto-oncogenes
Growth factors
Growth factor receptors
Intracellular transducers
Nuclear transcription factors

Tumor Suppressor Genes
Genes which cause cancer when both copies of gene are mutated in a diploid and lead to loss of function
Are recessive

p53
Tumor suppressor gene which is mutated in over 50% of all cases of human cancer
p53 was named by virtue of the mass of the protein, 53 kd

p53
is stabilized (by phosphorylation) upon DNA damage
Is a transcription factor

p53 target genes involved in cell cycle control:p21, WAF1/CIP1, GADD45, WIP1, mdm-2, EGFR, PCNA, CyclinD1, CyclinG, TGFalpha and 14-3-3sigma
p53 target genes involved in DNA repair:GADD45, PCNA, and p21 WAF1/CIP1
p53 target genes involved in apoptosis:BAX, Bcl-L, FAS1, FASL, IGF-BP3, PAG608 and DR5/KILLER, GML, P2XM
p53 target genes involved in angiogenesis:TSP-1, BAI1
p53 target genes involved in cellular stress response:TP53TG1, CSR, PIG3
Examples of genes targeted by p53:

p53
p21 is also known as cyclin dependent kinase inhibitor CDKN1A

p53 also induces transcription of genes involved in Programmed Cell Death (apoptosis)
-Turns on gene CD95 (APO-1/FAS) receptor
-can also activate pathway directly

The loss of p53 activity can thus be trebly dangerous in relation to cancer.  First, it allows faulty mutant cells to continue through the cell cycle.  Secondly, it allows them to escape apoptosis.  Third, it leads to the genetic instability characteristic of cancer cells, allowing further cancer-promoting mutations to accumulate as they divide.    Many other mutations can contribute to each of these types of misbehavior, but p53 mutations contribute to them all.
Alberts et al., 2002

Retinoblastoma
Gene product reversibly inhibits progression through cell-cycle
Hereditary Retinoblastoma
Sporadic Retinoblastoma

Major classes of genes that are responsible for cancer
Oncogenes (dominant)
Tumor Suppressor Genes (recessive)

Adenomatous polyposis coli (APC)
Polyps
Normal

Genetic Abnormalities
in Colorectal Cancer
Cancer is a Multistep Process
Tumors contain different genetic lesions
